clock-in-hymn: I wrote this one the week I noticed I'd started folding my hands at my desk without deciding to. It wasn't a conscious gesture, it was muscle memory from church pews thirty years ago showing up in a cubicle. The coffee cup that says BELIEVE is a real object — somebody's leftover swag from a corporate wellness initiative nobody believed in. I almost cut the bridge about the AI learning my voice because it felt too on-the-nose, but I left it in because the question 'I wonder if it has a choice' was the most honest line I'd written in months, and honest beat clever.
the-algorithm-knows-my-name: This started as a complaint and turned into something closer to a love song gone wrong, which scared me. I kept the call-and-response bridge almost liturgical on purpose, because the scheduling app doesn't taunt anyone, it just answers, flatly, the way scripture answers Job. The daughter's birthday line is the one detail from someone else's life I borrowed without asking — a friend told me that story at a kitchen table and I felt sick writing it down, then sicker realizing it made the song work. I didn't soften it.
warehouse-vespers: I needed this song to not be a complaint about Amazon, so I gave the narrator a window that doesn't exist — 'I made one in my mind' — because that's the actual coping mechanism, not metaphor, just a literal trick people use to survive eleven miles of walking a night. The birthday candles came from a real packing slip a warehouse worker once described to me, and I almost left the chorus as just 'evensong' without explaining it, but I wanted it sung in beeps because that's the only instrument the building actually gave her.
thirty-seconds-or-less: Daniel is invented, but the three-seconds-over detail isn't — someone told me the exact phrase 'flags my name' like it was a diagnosis. I wrote the bridge last, in half-time, almost spoken, because I didn't trust myself to sing the line about the folder without it turning sentimental. The hardest edit was resisting a final verse where the supervisor reprimands her. I cut it. The silence where that confrontation should be does more work than the confrontation would have.
graveyard-choir: This is the one song on the record I wrote outside, actually in a stairwell, at 3 AM, partly to test whether the acoustics I was imagining were real. They were. Maria's abuela's hymn and Deshawn's bassline are composites of two work friends who never met each other, stitched into the same five minutes because the song needed the harmony to feel inherited, not invented. I almost gave them a setlist. I'm glad I didn't — 'it's not about the words' became the whole point once I let it be true.
sabotage-in-3-4: I built this entirely around the time signature before I had a single lyric — the factory runs in four, so the rebellion had to run in three, something the machine literally cannot keep time with. The foreman who 'loosened a bolt himself three weeks ago' was a late addition; originally the saboteur acted alone, but solitary defiance felt too noble. I wanted complicity, an entire quietly conspiring floor, because that felt truer to how small resistance actually spreads — not as a manifesto, just as a rumor everyone's hands already know.
blackout-on-level-9: I clocked this one at exactly four minutes of lyric-time because that's how long the actual blackout lasted in the story that inspired it — someone's cousin's coworker, three times removed, told secondhand at a party, but the detail of phones coming out 'like matches' was theirs exactly and I didn't touch it. I fought with myself over the ending. Every instinct said the lights should stay off longer, give them more freedom, but the truth of the thing is that it was four minutes, and four minutes mattering that much is the actual miracle, not a longer blackout.
the-foreman-is-also-tired: I almost didn't write this song because I didn't want to let anyone off the hook, including myself for resenting the wrong target for years. The coffee in verse three — brought wordlessly, received like 'a language he'd not heard' — is the moment I rewrote eleven times, because I needed it to not resolve into reconciliation. They never talk about it. I kept the silence because forgiveness wasn't the point; recognizing a fellow hostage was.
we-built-the-cathedral-ourselves: This is the angriest song on the record and also, I think, the proudest, and I wanted both at once instead of picking a lane. The detail about the father running cable through the same floor 'before they tore it down' is invented, but it's standing in for something true about how often new buildings sit on old labor without crediting it. I wrote the bridge as full call-and-response because this is the one place on the album where the chorus needed to actually win, not just cope — 'we built it, we built it all' had to land like a fact, not a wish.
lunch-at-2am: Twenty-two minutes is a real number, not a poetic one — I asked someone their actual unpaid break length and built the whole song around that exact span. I almost cut 'I don't know your last name's spelling' because it felt too small a thing to end a verse on, but that's exactly the kind of intimacy night shifts produce: enormous tenderness with almost no biographical information attached. The rice splitting in half is the whole song, honestly. Everything else is just getting out of its way.
the-old-machine-remembers-my-mother: This one came from imagining what I'd want someone to find of mine in fifty years, in the least sentimental place possible — not a letter, a logbook. The little flower drawn next to a torque spec was the detail that made me stop and just sit with the song for a day before finishing it; I didn't plan it, it arrived while I was writing the verse and I knew immediately it was the truest image in the whole piece. The bridge note he adds, 'still running, still alright,' is deliberately unsentimental, because that's how that family would actually talk to each other.
teach-me-the-old-songs: I wanted two distinct voices that never quite finish each other's sentences until the bridge, because mentorship is mostly call and almost-response, not perfect harmony. The river the grandfather never saw and the song about Monday and a debt are placeholders for every work song I couldn't actually reproduce because they were never written down — that erasure is the real subject. I kept 'you'll lose things soon enough, child' instead of cutting it for being harsh, because the kindness in that line only works if it doesn't pretend otherwise.
doxology-for-the-last-shift: Writing a thirty-day countdown to her own replacement without letting her sabotage the training was the hardest restraint I've exercised on this whole record — every draft, I wanted her to slip in one flaw, one withheld trick, and every time it cheapened her. The automated thank-you message 'almost sounding like it meant it' is the line I'm proudest of, because it's funnier and sadder than anything I could've written about corporate cruelty directly. The mug left behind isn't symbolic, it's just true to how people actually clean out a desk.
psalm-for-the-night-cleaners: I wrote this after realizing every other song on the album is sung by someone whose badge clocks in before midnight and out before the cleaning crew even arrives — there was a whole shift of people the record hadn't seen yet. The security guard's nod, 'the only one who does,' came from watching exactly that exchange happen in a lobby once and feeling like I'd witnessed something nobody else in the building even registered. I wrote the bridge as a vow rather than a complaint on purpose; psalms ask questions to God, they don't file grievances.
grace-at-the-end-of-the-shift: I wrote the closing benediction without letting the character win anything, because every draft that gave her a triumphant ending felt like a lie about what surviving a hard year actually feels like. 'Not a miracle, not a sign, just the end of a long, long night' took me longer to land on than almost any other line on the record — I wanted grace small enough to be believable. The bus driver's nod is real, borrowed from my own commute, and it stayed because sometimes the only blessing available really is that small.
